# Green Building Design Specialist Interview Questions

###### Get Green Building Design Specialist Jobs Emailed to You

By signing up, you agree to the [terms of use](https://www.webscribble.com/tos)  and [privacy policy](https://www.webscribble.com/privacy-policy)

## 6 Interview Questions and Answers

These are the most common Green Building Design Specialist interview questions and how to answer them:

### 1. What inspired you to pursue a career in green building design?

I have always been passionate about sustainability and reducing our impact on the environment. I became interested in green building design as a way to combine my interests in architecture and environmental science, and have the opportunity to make a real difference in how we design and construct buildings.

### 2. What is your experience with LEED certification?

I have experience with the LEED certification process on several projects and have a good understanding of the credit categories and requirements. I have also taken the LEED Green Associate exam and have knowledge of the best practices for achieving LEED certification.

### 3. What is your experience with energy modeling and building simulation?

I have experience using various energy modeling and building simulation software such as eQuest, EnergyPlus, and DesignBuilder. I am familiar with the process of creating a building energy model, analyzing results, and using the information to optimize building performance.

### 4. What is your experience with sustainable building materials?

I have experience researching and specifying sustainable building materials for a variety of project types. I am familiar with the lifecycle assessment process and understand the importance of considering the entire lifecycle of a material when making selections.

### 5. What is your experience with daylighting and natural ventilation?

I have experience designing and analyzing daylighting and natural ventilation systems for buildings. I am familiar with the best practices for maximizing the use of natural light and air flow to improve building energy efficiency and comfort.

### 6. How do you stay current with the latest developments in green building design?

I stay current with the latest developments in green building design by reading industry publications, attending conferences and workshops, and participating in professional organizations such as the USGBC and the AIA. I also stay active on online discussion boards and communities to learn from other professionals in the field.

Are You An Employer?

**About Us**

Established in 1990, the Adventure Travel Trade Association is the largest global network of adventure travel leaders. Our community is made up of ~30,000 individual guides, tour operators, lodges, travel advisors, tourism boards, destination marketing and management organizations, outdoor educators, gear companies and travel media who share a belief and commitment to sustainable tourism. The connections and creativity of this vibrant community come together both virtually and in person to create and deliver the solutions that propel our businesses and our communities toward a responsible and profitable future.

![](https://www.sintali.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/sintali-web-logo-black-1.png)
![](https://www.sintali.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Photo_HPBS_Uzbekistan-900x675.jpg)

HPBS is an international engineering consulting company, providing EDGE Expert and EDGE auditing services as well as EDGE trainings through accredited EDGE Faculty across Europe and Central Asia.  Sustainability is at the heart of what HPBS does. We had the opportunity to sit down with Marina Kupriyanova, Deputy CEO and Co-founder of HPBS, to discuss the company’s journey, mission, and the crucial role of green building certifications in different regions.

**Julia Evans: To start, can you tell us a little about HPBS? When did you begin, and what’s your mission? What services do you offer?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** Of course! HPBS was founded in 2015, so we’re approaching a decade in the industry. We have three partners: Ilya Zavaleev, responsible for strategy development and innovational engineering; Anna Zavaleeva, leading PR, markets development and external communications; and myself, Marina Kupriyanova, leading products development and qualifications.

Before launching HPBS, we earned bright expertise at the international advisory company Ernst & Young. There we were first exposed to global green building practices. Our journey began with the LEED certification, an American system which impressed us with its focus on energy efficiency, indoor air quality, and high standards of design solutions. So, the first HPBS projects were LEED projects.

In 2017, we expanded our certification services to include BREEAM, a UK-based certification system, and later introduced WELL certification, which emphasizes on wellbeing including physical and mental health. In 2018 we started to dive into EDGE system, which provides a straightforward methodology focusing on three core areas: energy, water, and materials. In 2018 our team achieved the first EDGE expert certification.  The first project certified under the EDGE framework dates back to 2022.

Since 2021, we have extended our services to Germany, Uzbekistan, and Slovenia and have opened offices in Tashkent and Amsterdam. We currently have 25 employees spread across these regions.

**Julia Evans:** **Why are green buildings important globally, and particularly in the regions you operate in?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** Green buildings benefit developers, designers, and real estate professionals by enhancing energy and resource efficiency while improving comfort for occupants. The economic advantages are also clear — when buildings are energy efficient operational costs are significantly reduced. The second criterion, incorporating high quality design solutions and increased user comfort, leads to an increase in the asset value and its durability.

For example, in Uzbekistan, the government has increased its Paris Agreement commitment to reducing greenhouse gas emissions per GDP by 35% compared to 2010 levels. Green building certifications like EDGE provide measurable benchmarks that help companies meet their sustainability goals. In addition, in recent years, international banks and development agencies such as World Bank, IFC, ADB, EBRD, GIZ have focused their attention and resources on the development in Uzbekistan.

**Julia Evans: What are the challenges in scaling green buildings in regions like Uzbekistan?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** A significant challenge is the lack of awareness and expertise in measuring environmental impact. Many companies want to build sustainably but don’t know how to quantify their efforts. EDGE provides a structured approach to measurement, making sustainability tangible and actionable.

Financial institutions also play a key role. Several banks in Uzbekistan are making great efforts in green financing but lack clear methodologies to assess projects sustainability. EDGE certification helps bridge this gap by offering a standardized metric for green financing products.

**Julia Evans: Do you think the finance sector is crucial for accelerating green building adoption?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** Absolutely. However, in addition to the financial sector, government initiatives are essential. Uzbekistan has several sustainability programs supporting energy-efficient real estate projects and renewable energy adoption. These programs bring business and assets owners to a new stage of development: they set sustainable targets and implement incentives to pursue green certification.

**Julia Evans: How does EDGE contribute to the growth of green buildings?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** Education and knowledge sharing are key. To make sustainable changes, developers first need to understand what sustainability is and how to implement green building practices. We conduct EDGE Expert training sessions with the IFC (International Finance Corporation) to expand knowledge among industry. IFC, a member of the The World Bank Group, is the largest global development institution focused on supporting the private sector in emerging markets. In 2024, we hosted two trainings in Tashkent in 2025 one in Samarkand. Other events to spread EDGE knowledge are planned in other regions of Uzbekistan in 2025.

Business meetings are free for registered attendees, EDGE Expert Training sessions are affordable ($200 for a two-day session) and provide hands-on experience, ensuring participants leave with practical knowledge to improve their projects.

**Julia Evans: What is your vision for the future of green building in your regions?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:**

I will comment on two regions: Central Asia and countries from European Union.

Interest in green building has surged in the past few years. This is due to not only government initiatives but as well to international expertise coming to Uzbekistan. As international corporations introduce new initiatives, tools and best practices, external expert come with innovative expertise, local developers are more inclined to adopt these tools and implement energy-efficient and sustainable concepts. The combination of support from the government, efforts from voluntary policy makers and global expertise will drive to significant achievements in sustainable construction.

2025 has also been declared the “Year of Ecology” in Uzbekistan, signalling a strong governmental push towards sustainability. This initiative will likely accelerate green building adoption not just in Uzbekistan but across Central Asia.

Another situation is within the European Union. EU set a target of achieving climate neutrality by 2050. This has led to strict EU regulations on energy efficiency.

The EDGE standard is aligned with the EU Taxonomy as it focuses on the primary energy consumption of a building and a key compliance indicator of kWh/m²/year. For EDGE buildings the same indicator of primary energy consumption is used. The building owner in EU can compare the figures produced by the EDGE assessment tool with the EU Taxonomy figures.  This indicator makes it possible to understand how close the energy efficiency of the building to the models of EU Taxonomy and EU Energy Performance of Buildings Directive.

**Julia Evans: What role does HPBS play in connecting developers, financial institutions, and policymakers?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** HPBS serves as a bridge between these stakeholders. We connect banks with IFC (International Finance Corporation) and developers with best-in-class sustainability practices. By offering expertise beyond certification—such as energy modelling, climate risk assessments, and decarbonization strategies—we help clients achieve broader environmental goals.

One example is our work with SQB, a Client undergoing EDGE certification for 39 assets. We assist them with implementing their ESG strategy, greenhouse gas emissions calculations and strategic sustainability planning, demonstrating our holistic approach to sustainability consulting.

**Julia Evans: What are HPBS’s goals for the coming year?**

**Marina Kupriyanova:** We are an international company, and our goal is to continue expanding our expertise across borders. We aim to enhance knowledge transfer between countries, providing clients with top-tier sustainability solutions. Whether working with banks, developers, or policymakers, we strive to make sustainable building practices accessible, effective and measurable worldwide. Our mission is to create a favorable environment for people, nature, and business. The competencies of our team are rapidly evolving, and our services are becoming increasingly innovative, resource-efficient, and beneficial for humanity and the environment.

**Julia Evans: Thank you so much for your time Marina. We look forward to witnessing the growth of HPBS alongside the expanding presence of EDGE in the region.**

HPBS remains committed to driving sustainable building practices and ensuring that green certification becomes the norm rather than the exception. With a strong focus on education, collaboration, and innovative solutions, we look forward to working with them to shaper a greener future for the built environment.

If you are interested in finding out more about HPBS and contacting the team you can find them:

enriched_points.expanded_content: Conducting interviews with professionals provides insights into green building certification challenges, best practices, and current trends, enhancing understanding of industry standards and methodologies.

Surveys collect data from buildings with green certifications to assess performance metrics, occupant satisfaction, and operational efficiency, helping to refine certification processes and benchmarks.

Analyzing documentation from various certification bodies reveals criteria, compliance requirements, and methodologies, offering a comprehensive view of the standards needed for green building certifications.
